---
title: "Manual Test: plotlycsv"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, message=FALSE}
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

# For manual testing, you can use devtools or pak to load the package:
# devtools::load_all(".") 
# OR just library(plotlycsv) if already installed.
if (!requireNamespace("plotlycsv", quietly = TRUE)) {
  pkgload::load_all(".")
}
library(plotlycsv)

# Use global decorator to auto-detect filenames from chunk IDs/labels
use_plotly_csv()
```

## Introduction

This document is used to manually verify the `plotlycsv` functionality. 
Hover over any chart and look for the "Download CSV" icon (a downward arrow with a horizontal line).

## 1. Mtcars: Weight vs MPG (Chunk Label ID)

This chart's filename should default to `mtcars-plot.csv`.

```{r mtcars-plot}
p1 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) + 
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Mtcars Scatter Plot")

ggplotly(p1)
```

## 2. Iris: Sepal Dimensions (Figure Label ID)

This chart uses a figure label. The filename should prefer `fig-iris.csv` over the chunk name.

```{r}
#| label: fig-iris
#| fig-cap: "Iris Sepal dimensions"
p2 <- ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_light()

ggplotly(p2)
```

## 3. Economics: PCE over Time (Generic Chunk)

This chart has no explicit label. It should fall back to its autogenerated chunk name (or `unnamed-chunk-{n}.csv`).

```{r}
p3 <- ggplot(economics, aes(date, pce)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "US Personal Consumption Expenditures")

ggplotly(p3)
```

## 4. Diamonds: Cut Quality (Manual Override)

Even though the global decorator is active, we can manually override the filename for this specific plot.

```{r}
p4 <- ggplot(diamonds[1:1000, ], aes(cut, fill = cut)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_classic() +
  labs(title = "Diamond Cut Distribution (First 1000 rows)")

ggplotly(p4) |> 
  add_plotly_export_csv(filename = "diamonds_subset_manual.csv")
```

## 5. Multiple Traces (Mixed Data)

Testing how it handles multiple layers.

```{r mixed-traces}
p5 <- ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = class)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(title = "Engine Displacement vs Highway Miles")

ggplotly(p5)
```
